reasoning: The 10-year Treasury yield resolves based on the weekly WGS10YR series. The baseline for the forecast is the value corresponding to November 23, 2025, which represents the weekly average ending Friday, November 21. For that week, the daily DGS10 values were 4.13, 4.12, 4.13, 4.10, and ending at 4.06, which calculates to an average of about 4.11%. Because the daily yield closed much lower at 4.06% on Friday, the weekly average for the upcoming week ending Nov 28 (which resolves the Nov 30 date) begins with a significant mathematical disadvantage. Assuming a random walk with daily standard deviation ~0.05%, the weekly average has about a ~0.07% std dev. To increase above 4.11% from a 4.06% start by next week is statistically unlikely (estimated ~20-25%). For the subsequent resolution dates (December 23, 2025, and February 21, 2026), the forecast horizons of 1 and 3 months have greater variance (std dev ~0.21% and ~0.38% respectively). Recent market forecasts (like regular Reuters polls) predict the 10-year yield to hover around 4.10% over the next 3-6 months, while banks like UBS project gradual declines towards 3.75% by June 2026. Therefore, while probabilities revert closer to 50% for distant dates as uncertainty standard deviations widen, they remain slightly prejudiced against an increase due to the sub-baseline starting mean (4.06%) and mildly bearish market consensus over the near-term.
